text:
I am, by trade and by birth, a doormat. I accept this. What I did not accept, what no one consulted me about, was the word.

WELCOME. It says it right there across my chest, woven in, permanent, cheerful. Every morning the sun comes up and there it is, my one job, my creed, beaming up at boots that have, without exception, never once welcomed me anywhere.

Let me describe a typical day. A man approaches. Does he read my chest and feel a warmth, a sense of arrival, a fellowship between guest and home? He does not. He scrapes. He grinds the bottom of his shoe into my face like I am the problem and not, say, the forty yards of mud he chose to walk through. I am told this is my purpose. I am told I exist to receive the mud so the floor does not. I am, in other words, a martyr who was also forced to advertise the party I am dying for.

And the dog. We will not speak long about the dog, except to say that the dog has opinions about me, expresses them, and that no one has ever once apologized to me on the dog's behalf.

I have neighbors. The mat at number fourteen says GO AWAY and is beloved for it, photographed, complimented. Honesty, apparently, is charming when you are made of recycled rubber. I tell the truth too, but my truth was assigned to me at the factory and it is a lie, and somehow I am the one who looks insincere.

So this is my complaint, lodged with whoever sweeps the porch. I would like a sabbatical. I would like, just once, to be the one who is wiped off on something. And I would like my chest re-embroidered to read what I actually feel, which is simply: OH. IT'S YOU AGAIN.
